papers.title_en: Forget Without Compromise: Nexus Sampling for Streaming KV-Cache Eviction Under Fixed Budgets
papers.authors: Duc Duong, Hoang Anh Duy Le, Jianwen Xie, Anshumali Shrivastava, Zhaozhuo Xu
papers.evidence: 提出一种无需训练的KV缓存驱逐方法，适用于代理型LLM工作负载，与记忆管理相关
papers.tldr: 长上下文LLM推理中，现有KV缓存驱逐方法采用确定性top-K选择，导致细微重要token因单步注意力噪声被永久删除。本文提出Nexus Sampling，通过迭代Nexus评分发现桥接token，并用加权蓄水池采样以概率保留token。理论证明其长期存活性优于确定性top-K，实验在80%驱逐率下，LongBench精度达密集注意力的99%，检索任务性能超越基线，缓存内存最多降低10倍。
papers.abstract_en: Long-context and agentic LLM workloads push the KV cache past any fixed memory budget, forcing the inference stack to permanently evict tokens at every step of a continuous-inference stream. Existing methods all share the same template, a per-step direct-attention score followed by deterministic top-$K$ selection, which converts a single below-cutoff step into an irreversible verdict and permanently erases any subtly important token that direct attention cannot single out from noise. To address this challenge, we propose Nexus Sampling, a training-free eviction method that pairs Nexus scoring, an iterative walk over direct attention that surfaces bridge tokens, with weighted reservoir sampling, which retains tokens with inclusion probability in place of deterministic top-$K$. Theoretically, we show that Nexus Sampling dominates deterministic top-$K$ in long-run survival of subtly important tokens. Empirically, at 80% KV cache eviction, Nexus Sampling matches dense attention within 1% on LongBench while outperforming top-$K$ baselines on retrieval-heavy tasks, with up to 10x smaller per-sequence cache memory.

papers.title_en: A Stackelberg Framework for Resource-Aware LLM Agents: Learning, Repair, and Conditional Guarantees
papers.evidence: 处理LLM代理的资源分配，包括上下文/提示/工具使用，与预算下的记忆管理相关
papers.tldr: 大语言模型代理在多轮交互中面临有限计算资源下的上下文、提示和工具分配问题。静态阈值方法对不同任务和会话状态适应性差。本文提出基于Stackelberg博弈的框架，由控制器设定质量目标和成本激励，执行器响应资源行动。通过学习条件响应模型并优化策略，再通过真实API校准和投影进行修复。实验表明，相对于保守基线，该方法在300次评估中平均token成本降低17.4%，质量差异不显著。理论研究提供了均衡存在性和可迁移性的条件保证。
papers.abstract_en: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly operate as multi-turn systems that must allocate context, prompt verbosity, and tool access under finite computational budgets. Static thresholds are simple, but they are brittle under heterogeneous tasks and evolving session states. We formulate resource governance as a contextual Stackelberg game: a controller commits to a quality target and a cost incentive, while an executor responds with resource actions over context, prompting, and tool usage. We learn a conditional response model, optimize a leader policy against that model, and repair the resulting policy using real-API calibration and projection onto an empirically selected action set. For the restricted game, we establish conditional guarantees for equilibrium existence, follower-response stability, safe-set projection, and transfer from a surrogate environment to the real environment under bounded value error. The primary real-API experiment comprises 300 evaluated turns. Relative to a conservative baseline, the selected repaired controller reduces mean token cost by 17.4% (Welch $p=0.022$), while the measured quality difference is not statistically significant ($p=0.44$). The theoretical results are conditional and the experiments do not estimate their regret or transfer constants; consequently, the evidence establishes a promising repaired operating point, not a certified real-system equilibrium.
